title: MULTI_TENANT_SAAS_METRICS
value: Generate a multi-tenant SaaS metrics dataset. Include a metric fact table with fields (tenant_id, metric_name, timestamp, value) and at most 1 million rows, plus dimension tables for tenant, region, and plan. Most tenants should have low traffic and low metric volume, while a few large tenants ("whales") generate very high traffic following a Zipf-like distribution across tenant_id. Shared infrastructure issues, such as a database cluster degradation in a particular region, should cause correlated latency spikes and error-rate increases across multiple tenants in that region but not globally. Add tenant-specific incidents, such as misconfigurations or bad deployments, that affect only one tenant's metrics. Metric types should include request_rate, error_rate, latency_p50, latency_p95, cpu_usage, and memory_usage. The dataset should stress multi-tenant group-by aggregations, time-window analytics, and detection of shared versus isolated incidents.

title: ECOMMERCE_FRAUD
value: Generate a multi-currency e-commerce transaction dataset with at most 1 million transactions across fact tables for orders, payments, and fulfillment events. Include dimension tables for users, merchants, products, shipping addresses, payment methods, devices, and IP geolocation. Model sophisticated fraud patterns: account takeover rings where multiple accounts share device fingerprints and shipping addresses in temporal clusters; organized retail crime with bulk purchases of high-value items shipped to package forwarding services; synthetic identity fraud where new accounts exhibit unrealistic purchasing velocity. Introduce concept drift where fraud tactics evolve quarterly—early periods show simple card testing, later periods show complex triangulation fraud. Payment networks (Visa, Mastercard, regional processors) should have different approval rates and chargeback windows. Include legitimate power users whose behavior mimics fraud (high velocity, multiple addresses) but with consistent patterns. Currency exchange rates should fluctuate realistically, creating challenges for cross-border fraud amount thresholds. Seasonal shopping events (Black Friday, Singles Day) should show 10-100x transaction spikes with different fraud-to-legitimate ratios. The dataset should stress graph analytics for fraud ring detection, temporal pattern mining, imbalanced classification with <0.5% fraud rate, and real-time scoring under high throughput.

title: AUTONOMOUS_VEHICLE_SENSORS
value: Create a time-series dataset modeling sensor telemetry from a fleet of 10,000 autonomous vehicles over 6 months. Primary fact table contains at most 1 million sensor readings (10Hz sampling) with fields for vehicle_id, timestamp, sensor_type, and measurement vector. Sensor types include LIDAR point clouds (represented as aggregated features), radar tracks, camera object detections, GPS/IMU, and vehicle dynamics (speed, acceleration, steering angle). Dimension tables cover vehicle hardware configuration, geographic region, weather conditions, and road type. Model realistic sensor failure modes: GPS drift in urban canyons creating position uncertainty; LIDAR degradation in rain/fog reducing detection range; camera lens contamination gradually decreasing detection confidence; radar phantom tracks near metal structures. Introduce correlated failures where entire vehicle batches (same hardware revision) experience systematic sensor calibration drift after 100,000 km. Create scenarios requiring sensor fusion: pedestrian detected by camera but not LIDAR due to occlusion; stationary vehicles detected by LIDAR but filtered as noise until radar confirms. Include near-miss events where multiple sensors disagreed, requiring annotation of ground truth. Weather should correlate with sensor performance degradation and increased disengagement rates. The dataset should stress time-series similarity search, multimodal fusion logic, anomaly detection across sensor modalities, and high-cardinality group-by operations on vehicle configuration.

title: MICROSERVICES_OBSERVABILITY
value: Generate a distributed tracing and metrics dataset for a microservices architecture with 500 services. Include trace fact table with at most 1 million spans and fields for trace_id, span_id, parent_span_id, service_name, operation_name, start_time, duration, and status_code. Metrics fact table contains gauges and counters at 10-second resolution. Dimension tables for service, deployment version, infrastructure host, datacenter, and dependency graph. Model realistic distributed system behaviors: cascading failures where an upstream service degradation causes exponential timeout increases downstream; retry storms where client retries overwhelm a recovering service; thundering herd on cache invalidation causing synchronized database load; circuit breaker state transitions. Introduce deployment-induced incidents where canary releases of specific service versions show elevated error rates and latency. Create cross-datacenter replication lag scenarios where eventual consistency causes temporary data inconsistencies. Model resource exhaustion patterns: memory leaks causing gradual GC pause increases; connection pool exhaustion; thread pool saturation. Include long-tail latency where p99 is 100x p50 due to occasional database query plan regressions. Background noise should include health check traffic, batch jobs with different diurnal patterns, and A/B test traffic splits. The dataset should stress distributed trace reconstruction, causal inference for root cause analysis, service dependency graph analytics, and percentile aggregation across deployment dimensions.

title: FINANCIAL_MARKET_MICROSTRUCTURE
value: Create a high-frequency trading dataset modeling limit order book dynamics for 3,000 equities over one year. Order event fact table with at most 1 million events includes order submissions, modifications, cancellations, and executions with fields for symbol, timestamp (microsecond precision), order_id, price, quantity, side, and order_type. Trade fact table records executions. Dimension tables for symbol, exchange, sector, and market maker. Model realistic market microstructure: order flow toxicity where informed traders use aggressive orders before price moves; quote stuffing where participants submit and cancel orders rapidly to slow competitors; hidden liquidity via iceberg orders; internalization where retail flow never reaches lit exchanges. Introduce market structure events: trading halts triggered by volatility; short sale restrictions on specific symbols; circuit breakers causing synchronized trading pauses. Model the "flash crash" phenomenon where algorithmic selling creates temporary liquidity vacuums and cascading stop-loss triggers. Include realistic bid-ask spread dynamics that widen during low liquidity periods and tighten during high competition. Correlate volume and volatility with news events, earnings announcements, and macroeconomic releases. Create spoofing patterns where large non-bona fide orders are placed to mislead market participants then cancelled. The dataset should stress time-series reconstruction of order book state, market impact modeling, latency-sensitive analytics, and detection of manipulative trading patterns.

title: SMART_GRID_ENERGY
value: Generate a time-series dataset for a smart grid serving 5 million meters across residential, commercial, and industrial customers. Meter reading fact table with at most 1 million readings (15-minute intervals) includes meter_id, timestamp, active_power, reactive_power, voltage, and power_factor. Event fact table captures outages, voltage sags, and harmonics distortion. Dimension tables for customer, transformer, substation, feeder, and weather station. Model realistic grid dynamics: morning and evening residential demand peaks; commercial load synchronized with business hours; industrial base load with occasional high-draw manufacturing processes; solar PV generation with intermittent cloud-induced ramps; EV charging creating localized evening peaks. Introduce grid disturbances: voltage sags propagating through radial feeders during motor starts; cascading outages where substation failures cause downstream transformer overloads; capacitor bank switching causing temporary voltage spikes; imbalanced three-phase loads on residential transformers. Create non-technical loss patterns indicating theft: meters with consistently zero consumption in occupied buildings; consumption patterns inconsistent with building size; sudden consumption drops after utility inspection. Weather should strongly correlate with demand (HVAC load) and generation (solar irradiance). Include demand response events where large customers curtail load following grid operator signals. The dataset should stress spatial-temporal forecasting, anomaly detection for theft and equipment failure, load disaggregation, and constraint-aware optimization for distribution planning.

title: GENOMIC_VARIANTS
value: Create a genomic variant calling dataset from whole-genome sequencing of 100,000 individuals across diverse populations. Variant fact table with at most 1 million variants includes chromosome, position, reference_allele, alternate_allele, genotype, quality_score, read_depth, and allele_frequency. Annotation dimension tables for gene, transcript, functional consequence (missense, nonsense, synonymous), population, and phenotype. Model realistic genetic architecture: common variants (MAF > 5%) follow Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium; rare variants show population stratification and founder effects; de novo mutations in parent-offspring trios; structural variants (CNVs, inversions) spanning kilobases. Include sequencing artifacts: systematic errors in homopolymer regions; reference genome bias causing under-calling of variants in highly divergent haplotypes; batch effects where different sequencing centers show technology-specific error profiles. Create disease association patterns: GWAS hits with small effect sizes requiring large samples; Mendelian disease variants with complete penetrance; polygenic risk scores aggregating thousands of variants; epistatic interactions where variant combinations show non-additive effects. Model linkage disequilibrium creating correlation structure across nearby variants. Include pharmacogenomic variants affecting drug metabolism. Ancestry should create population-specific allele frequencies and linkage patterns. The dataset should stress variant quality filtering, population genetics inference, gene-based association testing, and joins between variants and phenotypes with complex inheritance patterns.

title: SUPPLY_CHAIN_NETWORK
value: Generate a multi-echelon supply chain dataset covering 10,000 SKUs flowing through suppliers, manufacturing plants, distribution centers, and retail locations globally. Shipment fact table with at most 1 million shipments includes origin, destination, SKU, quantity, ship_date, delivery_date, and transport_mode. Inventory fact table captures daily stock levels at each location. Dimension tables for SKU, location, carrier, and bill of materials. Model realistic supply chain dynamics: bullwhip effect where demand variability amplifies upstream; just-in-time replenishment for high-velocity SKUs; safety stock for demand uncertainty; lot-sizing creating stepped replenishment patterns. Introduce disruptions: port congestion causing container dwell times to triple; semiconductor shortage creating allocation constraints; geopolitical events closing specific supply routes; natural disasters affecting regional suppliers. Create demand patterns: seasonal products (e.g., holiday goods) with single annual peak; fashion items with short life cycles and obsolescence; staple goods with steady demand; promotional lifts during marketing campaigns. Model production constraints: assembly line changeovers creating setup costs; shared bottleneck resources limiting throughput; quality defects requiring rework or scrap. Include trade-offs between transportation modes (ocean slow/cheap, air fast/expensive). Lead times should vary stochastically and increase under supply chain stress. The dataset should stress network flow optimization, multi-period inventory planning, scenario analysis under disruption, and bill of materials explosion for demand propagation.

title: DIGITAL_AD_ATTRIBUTION
value: Create a multi-touch attribution dataset tracking user journeys from awareness to conversion across digital channels. Impression fact table with at most 1 million impressions includes user_id, campaign_id, channel, creative_id, timestamp, and cost. Click and conversion fact tables record downstream actions. Dimension tables for campaign, creative, audience segment, publisher, and product. Model realistic customer journeys: awareness-stage display ads followed by consideration-stage social media retargeting and conversion-stage search ads; cross-device journeys where users browse on mobile and convert on desktop; multi-session journeys spanning weeks with view-through and click-through interactions. Introduce attribution challenges: organic brand search that should credit earlier touchpoints; direct traffic masking referral sources; cookie deletion creating apparent user discontinuities; walled garden platforms (e.g., social media) providing aggregated reporting without user-level data. Create campaign interference where overlapping campaigns cannibalize each other versus incrementality. Model seasonality and trend: summer travel campaigns, back-to-school shopping, holiday gifting. Include incrementality experiments (geo holdouts, PSA tests) providing ground truth for lift measurement. Creative fatigue should show diminishing returns as users see ads repeatedly. Different channels should have different latency to conversion (search converts fast, display requires nurturing). The dataset should stress multi-touch attribution modeling, incrementality measurement, cross-device identity resolution, and causal inference under confounding.

title: TELECOM_NETWORK_PLANNING
value: Generate a cellular network dataset covering 50,000 cell sites serving 10 million subscribers over a metropolitan area. Radio measurement fact table with at most 1 million measurements includes subscriber_id, cell_id, timestamp, signal_strength (RSRP), signal_quality (SINR), throughput, and connection_state. Call detail records capture voice calls, data sessions, and handovers. Dimension tables for cell site, subscriber plan, device type, and geographic region. Model realistic radio propagation: signal attenuation with distance following path loss models; shadowing from buildings creating coverage holes; interference from neighboring cells; weather effects (rain fade at high frequencies). Introduce mobility patterns: commuter flows creating cell breathing (expansion/contraction of coverage); stadium events causing extreme density spikes; highway traffic creating rapid handovers. Model network congestion: resource block utilization increasing latency and reducing throughput; admission control rejecting connections at capacity; load balancing distributing users across carriers. Create device heterogeneity: legacy 4G devices, 5G smartphones, IoT modules with different capability and traffic profiles. Include subscriber behavior: heavy users consuming disproportionate bandwidth; video streaming dominating traffic; gaming requiring low latency. Cell sites should have different capacity (macro cells, small cells, indoor DAS) and backhaul constraints (fiber, microwave). Planned network upgrades (new sites, spectrum refarming) should improve performance in specific areas after deployment. The dataset should stress geospatial analytics, coverage and capacity optimization, subscriber churn prediction, and radio resource management simulation.

title: CLOUD_COST_ALLOCATION
value: Create a cloud infrastructure usage dataset for a large organization with 1,000 teams consuming resources across compute, storage, networking, and managed services. Resource usage fact table with at most 1 million resource-hours includes resource_id, resource_type, team_id, project_id, timestamp, usage_quantity, and cost. Commitment and reservation fact tables track reserved instances, savings plans, and committed use discounts. Dimension tables for team, cost center, cloud provider, region, and resource tag taxonomy. Model realistic cloud consumption: development environments with bursty usage during work hours; production workloads with steady baseline and traffic-driven autoscaling; batch processing jobs with scheduled spikes; data pipelines with cascading downstream dependencies. Introduce cost optimization patterns: rightsizing over-provisioned instances; spot instances for fault-tolerant workloads; reserved instances amortized over time; tiered storage with lifecycle policies moving cold data to cheaper tiers. Create shared resource allocation challenges: multi-tenant databases requiring cost splitting; shared networking infrastructure; platform services consumed by multiple teams. Model commitment utilization: unused reserved instance hours representing wasted spend; savings plans not covering all usage; commitment expiration requiring renewals. Include tagging inconsistencies where resources lack proper cost center labels, requiring heuristic allocation. Cross-region data transfer costs should create optimization opportunities. Sudden cost spikes should result from misconfigurations (e.g., infinite loops, runaway autoscaling). The dataset should stress hierarchical cost rollup, commitment recommendation, anomaly detection for cost spikes, and multi-dimensional showback reporting with complex allocation rules.

title: ENTITY_BOUNDARY_AMBIGUITY
value: We track customer interactions. Each interaction has a session ID, user agent string, IP address, geolocation (country, city, lat/lon), device type, OS version, browser version, screen resolution, and referrer URL. Interactions include page views with URL and title, clicks with element ID and coordinates, form submissions with field names and values, and API calls with endpoint and response time.

title: SELF_REFERENTIAL_FOREIGN_KEYS
value: Build an organizational hierarchy. Employees have an ID, name, email, and salary. Each employee reports to exactly one manager (who is also an employee), except for the CEO who reports to no one. Employees can manage multiple direct reports. Track the reporting chain depth (levels from CEO).

title: MANY_TO_MANY_RELATIONSHIPS
value: Students enroll in courses. Each student has an ID, name, and major. Each course has an ID, name, and credit hours. A student can enroll in multiple courses, and a course can have multiple students. Track the enrollment date and grade for each student-course pair.

title: TEMPORAL_CAUSALITY
value: Track user subscriptions. Users sign up on a signup_date. Subscriptions start on start_date (always >= signup_date) and can be cancelled on cancel_date (always >= start_date if present). Calculate subscription duration in days as cancel_date - start_date (or NULL if active). Track whether the subscription is currently active (active if cancel_date is NULL).

title: MULTI_LEVEL_FOREIGN_KEY_CHAIN
value: Model a supply chain: Suppliers provide raw materials. Manufacturers use raw materials to produce components. Assemblers use components to build products. Distributors sell products to retailers. Retailers sell to customers. Track the full lineage: which customer purchase came from which supplier.

title: AMBIGUOUS_DOMAIN_LANGUAGE
value: Build a schema for high-frequency trading. Track order flow toxicity, quote stuffing events, and iceberg orders. Model the order book with bid/ask spread dynamics. Include market maker rebates, payment for order flow (PFOF), and internalization. Track maker/taker fees and liquidity provision.

title: MASSIVE_SCHEMA
value:
Create a complete enterprise resource planning (ERP) system schema covering:

HR: employees, departments, positions, salaries, benefits, performance reviews, time off requests
Finance: general ledger, accounts, transactions, invoices, payments, budgets, cost centers
Sales: leads, opportunities, quotes, orders, customers, contacts, territories, commissions
Inventory: warehouses, locations, stock levels, transfers, adjustments, cycle counts
Manufacturing: work orders, bill of materials, routings, production schedules, quality checks
Procurement: vendors, purchase requisitions, purchase orders, receipts, vendor invoices
CRM: accounts, contacts, activities, campaigns, email tracking, call logs
Support: tickets, knowledge base articles, SLAs, escalations, customer feedback
Analytics: dashboards, reports, KPIs, data warehouse dimensions and facts

Each module should have 5-7 fully normalized tables with proper relationships.

title: CIRCULAR_DEPENDENCIES
value: Model a social network graph. Users can follow other users. Track mutual follows (user A follows user B AND user B follows user A). For each user, calculate their follower count, following count, and follower-to-following ratio. The ratio must be computed before assigning user popularity tier (micro, mid, macro, mega) which affects their default follower count when they sign up.

title: POLYMORPHIC_RELATIONSHIPS
value:
Build a notification system. Users receive notifications. A notification can be about different things:
- A comment on a post (references posts table)
- A like on a photo (references photos table)
- A friend request (references users table)
- A message (references messages table)

Each notification has a type, target entity, and timestamp. Users can mark notifications as read.

title: MISSING_CRITICAL_INFORMATION
value: Track product reviews. Reviews are submitted by users.

title: IMPLICIT_DERIVED_TABLES
value: Track individual transactions. Each transaction has user_id, amount, timestamp. Calculate daily revenue per user. Build a leaderboard showing top 100 users by total lifetime revenue.

title: EPIDEMIOLOGICAL_CONTACT_TRACING
value: Model a contact tracing system for infectious disease spread across a metro area with 2 million people over 18 months. Core fact tables: encounters (person_a, person_b, location_id, timestamp, duration_minutes, proximity_category), symptom_reports (person_id, timestamp, symptoms_bitmask, severity), test_results (person_id, timestamp, test_type, result, viral_load). Dimension tables for person (age_group, occupation, household_size), location (venue_type, ventilation_quality, capacity), and test facility. Generation time follows Gamma(5.5, 0.9) distribution for time from person A's infection to person B's infection through that contact chain. Serial interval for symptom onset follows right-skewed distribution with occasional negative values for presymptomatic transmission. Basic reproduction number R₀ varies by location type: nightclubs 8-12, offices 2-4, outdoor spaces 0.5-1.5, with time-varying effective R(t) based on intervention policies. False negative rate for tests depends on days since exposure: approximately 100% on day 0, drops to 40% on day 4, rises back to 70% after day 14. Household attack rate follows Beta(8, 2) distribution conditional on ventilation quality and household size with saturation effects in small apartments. Superspreading events follow overdispersed negative binomial where 10% of cases cause 80% of transmissions requiring heterogeneous mixing model. Incubation period follows Weibull(5.8, 2.3) but left-truncated for confirmed cases due to surveillance bias. Contact network degree distribution follows power law in social hubs but Poisson in residential areas creating mixture model by location type. Dataset should stress self-referential person-to-person transmission chains creating directed acyclic graphs, temporal validity windows for infectiousness period, probabilistic linkage inferring transmission pairs from overlapping space-time proximity without ground truth, and bipartite graphs of people-to-locations for indirect contact inference.

title: WILDFIRE_PROPAGATION_SUPPRESSION
value: Model wildfire behavior across 500,000 hectares of forest over fire season. Fire perimeter fact table with at most 1 million records includes fire_id, timestamp, geometry_polygon, fire_intensity, rate_of_spread, containment_percentage. Resource deployment table covers crew_id, vehicle_id, timestamp, location, assigned_fire_segment, suppression_action. Dimension tables for vegetation fuel type, topography (elevation, slope, aspect), weather station readings (temperature, humidity, wind_speed, wind_direction), and suppression resource capabilities. Fire spread rate follows log-normal distribution with strong conditional dependencies increasing exponentially with wind speed via Rothermel model, multiplied by aspect factor where south-facing slopes burn 3x faster, and exponential decay with fuel moisture content. Spotting distance for embers jumping containment lines follows heavy-tailed Pareto distribution where most spots land within 100 meters but 1% travel beyond 2 kilometers with probability increasing super-linearly with wind speed. Containment probability per fire segment follows logistic function of cumulative suppression effort hours, fuel type flammability, and current fire intensity. Crew fatigue accumulates as Markov process where effectiveness decays exponentially with consecutive shift hours but resets with rest periods, affecting both productivity and injury risk which follows Weibull hazard function. Wind direction changes follow von Mises circular distribution causing fire shape to evolve from elliptical to irregular polygons. Fuel load regeneration after prescribed burns follows Gompertz growth curve over decades. Aircraft deployment effectiveness depends on joint distribution of visibility (log-normal), wind speed (Weibull), and drop accuracy (bivariate normal centered on target with wind-direction bias). Back-burning success probability follows Bayesian update combining prior fuel moisture with real-time wind observations. Dataset should stress spatio-temporal polygon evolution, optimization under resource constraints, Monte Carlo simulation for fire behavior prediction, and causal inference for suppression tactic effectiveness with confounding by fire severity.

title: INSURANCE_CLAIMS_SUBROGATION
value: Generate insurance claims dataset for 500,000 policies across auto, property, and liability lines over 5 years. Claims fact table with at most 1 million claims includes policy_id, claimant_id, loss_date, report_date, claim_type, estimated_loss, paid_amount, and claim_status. Subrogation recovery table tracks attempts to recover costs from at-fault third parties. Dimension tables for policy, insured party, coverage limits, adjuster, legal representation, and third party. Claim frequency follows Poisson process with intensity varying by policyholder risk score, but zero-inflated for no-claims-discount incentive creating hurdle model where policyholders delay reporting minor claims. Claim severity follows mixture of log-normal for typical claims and Pareto for catastrophic tail events exceeding policy limits. Time from loss to report follows Weibull distribution with shape parameter varying by claim type: auto claims reported within days (shape > 3), property damage within weeks (shape around 2), liability claims potentially years later (shape < 1). Fraud probability follows logistic regression on policyholder tenure, claim timing relative to policy inception, claimant network connections detected via shared addresses or bank accounts, and claim amount just below investigation threshold. Subrogation recovery amount given liability established follows beta distribution of fault percentage multiplied by total damages, but recovery success depends on third party solvency following survival analysis. Claims clustering occurs in catastrophic events (hurricanes, hailstorms) creating spatial dependence via Gaussian process, or temporal clustering in staged accident rings. Legal cost follows geometric Brownian motion with drift toward settlement and volatility increasing with case complexity. Medical claim severity inflation follows compound Poisson process where underlying injury count is Poisson and each injury cost is gamma-distributed. Dataset should stress survival analysis for claim closure time, mixture models separating fraud from legitimate claims, spatial point process for catastrophe modeling, and causal inference for adjuster intervention effectiveness controlling for selection bias.

title: QUANTUM_COMPUTING_CALIBRATION
value: Create a dataset for quantum computer calibration across 1,000 qubits over continuous operation. Measurement fact table with at most 1 million records includes qubit_id, timestamp, gate_operation, gate_fidelity, T1_relaxation_time, T2_coherence_time, readout_error, and crosstalk_matrix. Control pulse fact table logs pulse_sequence_id, qubit_id, pulse_amplitude, pulse_duration, detuning_frequency. Dimension tables for qubit physical position on chip, coupling topology, control line routing, and environmental conditions (temperature, magnetic field, vibration). Single-qubit gate fidelity follows beta distribution Beta(200, 1) for well-calibrated qubits achieving 99.5% typical fidelity, but systematic drift over hours follows Ornstein-Uhlenbeck process with mean reversion toward optimal parameters. T1 relaxation time follows log-normal distribution LN(50μs, 0.3) but exhibits diurnal temperature-dependent variation creating cyclical pattern. Two-qubit gate fidelity degrades with qubit distance following exponential decay and crosstalk interference modeled as multivariate normal with covariance structure determined by coupling graph topology. Readout error follows binomial distribution but with state-dependent asymmetry where |0⟩ misclassified as |1⟩ occurs at rate 2-4% while reverse error occurs at 1-2%, creating confusion matrix that evolves with readout pulse calibration. Cosmic ray events causing bit flips follow Poisson process with intensity 1 per hour per chip creating sudden fidelity drops detectable as outliers. Qubit frequency drift follows random walk with occasional jumps when charge traps switch states, requiring real-time frequency tracking via Kalman filter. Cross-resonance gate error depends on joint spectral crowding following Lorentzian lineshape interference between neighboring qubits. Leakage to non-computational states follows absorbing Markov chain where probability increases with gate sequence depth. Dataset should stress time-series forecasting of calibration drift, network effect modeling of crosstalk propagation through coupling graph, anomaly detection for hardware faults, and optimal experiment design for efficient characterization of 1000-dimensional error parameter space.

title: PHARMACEUTICAL_CLINICAL_TRIAL_ADAPTIVE
value: Generate dataset for adaptive Phase III clinical trial with 10,000 patients across 200 sites testing drug efficacy for heterogeneous disease. Patient enrollment fact table with at most 1 million records includes patient_id, site_id, enrollment_date, baseline_covariates (biomarkers, demographics, disease_severity), treatment_arm, and dosage. Outcome measurement table tracks patient_id, visit_date, primary_endpoint, secondary_endpoints, adverse_events, and compliance_percentage. Dimension tables for site, investigator, drug formulation, and protocol amendment. Patient enrollment rate follows non-homogeneous Poisson process with intensity varying by site activation date and seasonal effects creating enrollment competition between trials. Treatment effect size follows mixture distribution where 60% of patients are non-responders (effect size 0), 30% are moderate responders (effect size Gaussian with mean 0.3 standard deviations), and 10% are strong responders (effect size >1 SD), with mixture component membership predicted by baseline biomarker latent class. Dropout hazard follows Cox proportional hazards model where hazard increases with adverse event severity, travel distance to site, and inverse of perceived treatment benefit creating informative censoring. Protocol deviations follow Markov chain where sites in violation state have increased probability of future violations unless corrective action taken. Adverse event onset time follows Weibull distribution with shape parameter depending on drug mechanism, and severity follows ordinal logistic regression on cumulative dose exposure. Placebo response follows time-dependent pattern with initial improvement from enrollment (regression to mean and placebo effect) then gradual decline. Site effects follow hierarchical model where site-level random intercepts are correlated with geographic region creating spatial structure. Interim analysis triggers adaptive randomization where allocation ratio shifts toward better-performing arms using Thompson sampling, but treatment effect estimates are biased by selection creating inferential challenges. Missing data mechanism is missing at random conditional on last observed outcome creating pattern mixture model requirement. Dataset should stress survival analysis with informative censoring, hierarchical mixed effects models with crossed random effects for patients and sites, Bayesian adaptive design simulation, and causal inference for treatment effect heterogeneity using instrumental variables or g-computation for time-varying confounding.
